commented_code:
number of messages initially. Thus the tasks may not be fairly distributed
 to the workers.
 
-To disable prefetching, set :setting:`worker_prefetch_multiplier` to 1.
-Changing that setting to 0 will allow the worker to keep consuming
-as many messages as it wants.
+To limit the broker to only deliver one message per process at a time,
+set :setting:`worker_prefetch_multiplier` to 1. Changing that setting to 0
+will allow the worker to keep consuming as many messages as it wants.
+
+If you need to completely disable broker prefetching while still using
+early acknowledgments, enable :setting:`worker_disable_prefetch`.
+When this option is enabled the worker only fetches a task from the broker
+when one of its processes is available.
+
+You can also enable this via the :option:`--disable-prefetch <celery worker --disable-prefetch>`
+command line flag.
 
 For more on prefetching, read :ref:`optimizing-prefetch-limit`
 
 .. note::
 
     Tasks with ETA/countdown aren't affected by prefetch limits.
 
+.. setting:: worker_disable_prefetch
+

commented_code:
Also when running Celery beat embedded (:option:`-B <celery worker -B>`)
 on Jython as a thread the max interval is overridden and set to 1 so
 that it's possible to shut down in a timely manner.
+
+.. setting:: beat_cron_starting_deadline_seconds
+
+``beat_cron_starting_deadline_seconds``

commented_code:
@transaction.atomic
     def create_article(request):
         article = Article.objects.create()
-        transaction.on_commit(lambda: expand_abbreviations.delay(article.pk))
+        expand_abbreviations.delay_on_commit(article.pk)
+        return HttpResponseRedirect('/articles/')
+
+This method was added in Celery 5.4. It's a shortcut that uses Django's
+``on_commit`` callback to launch your Celery task once all transactions
+have been committed successfully.
+
+With Celery <5.4
+~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

discussion_comments.commented_code:
@@ -2054,7 +2054,31 @@ once all transactions have been committed successfully.
     @transaction.atomic
     def create_article(request):
         article = Article.objects.create()
-        transaction.on_commit(lambda: expand_abbreviations.delay(article.pk))
+        expand_abbreviations.delay_on_commit(article.pk)
+        return HttpResponseRedirect('/articles/')
+
+This method was added in Celery 5.4. It's a shortcut that uses Django's
+``on_commit`` callback to launch your Celery task once all transactions
+have been committed successfully.
+
+With Celery <5.4
+~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
discussion_comments.comment_body: Left the old instructions for earlier versions of Celery, but perhaps we can entirely remove the old way since the docs for the older versions are accessible as e.g. https://docs.celeryq.dev/en/v5.3.6/userguide/tasks.html

discussion_comments.commented_code:
@@ -2054,7 +2054,31 @@ once all transactions have been committed successfully.
     @transaction.atomic
     def create_article(request):
         article = Article.objects.create()
-        transaction.on_commit(lambda: expand_abbreviations.delay(article.pk))
+        expand_abbreviations.delay_on_commit(article.pk)
+        return HttpResponseRedirect('/articles/')
+
+This method was added in Celery 5.4. It's a shortcut that uses Django's
+``on_commit`` callback to launch your Celery task once all transactions
+have been committed successfully.
+
+With Celery <5.4
+~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
discussion_comments.comment_body: It’s not bad to say that in an older version it behaves differently, but I agree with your POV/logic.

commented_code:
:signature: ``worker-offline(hostname, timestamp, freq, sw_ident, sw_ver, sw_sys)``
 
 The worker has disconnected from the broker.
+
+Mailbox Configuration (Advanced)
+--------------------------------
+
+Celery uses `kombu.pidbox.Mailbox` internally to send control and broadcast commands
+to workers.
+
+.. versionadded:: Kombu 5.6.0
